description: The effect algebra has laws (monad laws, handler composition laws, effect interaction properties). Add StreamData or PropCheck property-based tests to verify these hold across edge cases. This increases confidence in the core computation framework and catches subtle composition bugs that example-based tests miss.
title: Add property-based tests for effect algebra laws

description: Audit the public API surface, deprecate experimental effects, and move toward a 1.0 release. The core (Comp, State, Reader, Writer, Throw, Bracket, Yield) is solid. Identify which effects are stable vs experimental, document stability guarantees, and create a migration path for any breaking changes.
title: Stabilize API surface toward 1.0

description: The benchmarks exist but could be more systematic. Profile: FiberPool under concurrent load, Channel under sustained backpressure, Brook with large streams, DB batching with high concurrency. Identify bottlenecks and establish performance regression baselines.
title: Systematic performance profiling under load

description:
Extend the persistence effects to integrate write operations with the existing IBatchable read batching, adding a transparent write-through cache. Currently DB.Batch eliminates N+1 reads via automatic batching, but repeat reads for the same entity still hit the DB. A cache layer would eliminate redundant reads entirely within a computation scope.

The handler would:
1. Wrap the existing DB handler
2. Maintain a cache as handler state (like State effect)
3. Intercept reads: check cache, batch misses via IBatchable, cache results
4. Intercept writes: write through to DB, update/invalidate cache
5. Scope naturally per-computation (cleanup via handler scope)

Prior art:

- Haxl (Facebook, Haskell): Marlow et al. 2014 'There is no Fork'. Applicative-style automatic batching with per-request cache. Reads are batched AND cached transparently. The theoretical foundation — Skuld's DB.Batch already partially implements the batching side, adding the cache completes the picture.

- DataLoader (Facebook, JS/GraphQL): Practical incarnation of Haxl. Per-request batching + caching for GraphQL resolvers. Batches all reads in a tick, caches results, subsequent requests for the same key return from cache. Widely adopted (Absinthe.DataLoader in Elixir).

- Identity Map + Unit of Work (Fowler, Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture): Identity Map ensures each entity is loaded once per unit of work. Unit of Work tracks all mutations and coordinates write-back. Together they give write-through cache semantics.

- Ecto does NOT do this — preloads batch but don't cache. No identity map. Each Repo.get(User, 1) hits the DB even if the same entity was just loaded.

Skuld's effect handler architecture makes this naturally composable — the handler has complete control over how read/write effects are interpreted, and scoping gives automatic cache lifecycle management.
title: Write-through cache layer integrating DB writes with batched reads

description: Command, ChangeEvent, and EventAccumulator are domain-specific (CQRS/event-sourcing patterns) rather than general-purpose algebraic effects. Extract them to a separate package (e.g. skuld_domain or skuld_cqrs) to keep the core library lean and focused on foundational effects.
title: Extract domain-specific effects to separate package

description: Invest in better error messages when: handlers are missing from evidence at runtime, comp blocks are malformed or misused, effect stacks don't compose correctly. Good error messages are critical for adoption — users need to understand what went wrong and how to fix it, especially with macro-heavy code where compile-time errors can be opaque.
title: Improve error messages for missing handlers and malformed comp blocks

Rig identity bead for skuld.

state: active
title: skuld

description: The README is comprehensive but dense (85KB). Create a tutorial/cookbook showing common patterns: web request handler with effects, background job with error handling and retries, streaming pipeline with Brook, testing effectful code with test handlers. Lower the barrier for developers new to algebraic effects.
title: Add newcomer-friendly documentation: tutorials and cookbook

description: The replay/resume capability via EffectLogger is powerful. Extend it toward durable execution: persist effect logs to storage, resume computations across process restarts, handle partial replay with changed handlers. This would be a significant differentiating feature for long-running workflows and saga patterns.
title: Push EffectLogger toward durable execution
